title: Abstract Classes
description: C++'s formal enforcement of abstraction: pure virtual functions, classes that can't be instantiated, and forcing derived classes to implement an interface.
objectives: Understand why an abstract class cannot be instantiated
Force derived classes to implement a shared interface
Combine abstract classes with the polymorphism from Virtual Functions

sections.text: 1. Recap: Abstraction Needs a Formal Tool

Abstraction discussed hiding complexity behind a simple interface, informally. Abstract classes are C++'s formal, compiler-enforced way to define an interface that every derived class is required to implement.
A pure virtual function, written with = 0, has no implementation in the base class at all — it exists only to declare that every derived class must provide its own implementation.
This builds directly on virtual from Virtual Functions — but = 0 goes a step further, saying "this function has no default behavior whatsoever, and any usable class must supply one."
3. A Class with Any Pure Virtual Function Is Abstract

sections.code: Shape s; // ERROR — cannot instantiate an abstract class

sections.text: A class containing at least one pure virtual function becomes an abstract class, and C++ refuses to let you create an object of it directly — there's no sensible area() to call if one were created, since Shape itself never defined one. Shape exists purely to be inherited from.
4. Forcing Derived Classes to Implement It

If Circle failed to implement area(), Circle would itself remain abstract too (having inherited an unimplemented pure virtual function), and Circle c; would also fail to compile. This is the enforcement: the compiler guarantees no concrete Shape-derived object can exist without a working area().

This is the real payoff, combining Arrays, Virtual Functions, and Abstract Classes: a single array of Shape* can hold any mix of shapes, and shapes[i]->area() automatically runs the correct implementation for whichever specific shape each pointer actually refers to.

sections.title: A job contract requiring a specific duty
sections.text: A job contract can require "must be able to drive" without specifying which particular car the employee drives — that's the equivalent of a pure virtual function: a required capability with no default way to fulfill it built in. You can't hire someone under a contract that's just a list of unfulfilled requirements (an abstract Shape); you hire an actual person (a Circle or Rectangle) who has actually fulfilled that requirement in their own specific way.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to create an object of a class that has any unimplemented pure virtual function — the compiler blocks it, since it's an abstract class.

Forgetting = 0 and just writing virtual double area(); with no body — that's an ordinary virtual function needing implementation somewhere, not a pure virtual one requiring every derived class to supply it.
Forgetting to implement a pure virtual function in a derived class — the derived class remains abstract too, and still can't be instantiated.
Missing that override still applies here exactly as in ordinary virtual functions — it's good practice to keep using it when implementing a pure virtual function.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a pure virtual function?
questions.answer: A virtual function with no implementation in the base class, declared with = 0, requiring every derived class to provide its own implementation.

questions.question: What makes a class abstract?
questions.answer: Containing at least one pure virtual function — such a class cannot be instantiated directly.

questions.question: Can you create an object of an abstract class?
questions.answer: No — the compiler refuses to let you instantiate a class that has any unimplemented pure virtual function.

questions.question: What happens if a derived class doesn't implement all of its base class's pure virtual functions?
questions.answer: The derived class remains abstract as well, and also cannot be instantiated until every inherited pure virtual function is implemented.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A pure virtual function, declared with = 0, has no implementation in its base class at all, and any class containing one becomes an abstract class that cannot be instantiated directly — it exists purely to be inherited from. This formally enforces abstraction: every concrete derived class is compiler-required to supply its own implementation of that function, guaranteeing no usable object exists without one. Combined with the runtime polymorphism from Virtual Functions, this lets code work with a collection of a base type, like Shape*, and correctly call each object's own specific implementation, such as area(), without needing to know exactly which derived type each one actually is.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Abstract Classes
sections.text: = 0 = a required duty with no default way to do it. Any class with one becomes abstract — can't be hired (instantiated) directly. Derived classes must actually fulfill the duty before they're usable.
